critic_raw:
This design compares a hybrid CNN-RNN against logistic regression for hospital readmission prediction.

The fundamental experimental confound is that the CNN-RNN processes raw temporal sequences of treatments and procedures while the logistic regression receives only hand-engineered summary features. The design explicitly acknowledges this: 'The baseline receives equal tuning effort: hyperparameter search over regularization strength (C), class weight balancing to handle imbalance, and identical preprocessing on structured features.' But the information disparity is not just a feature set difference — the CNN-RNN is operating over the full temporal sequence while the logistic regression has access only to summary statistics derived from that sequence. Any observed performance difference could be entirely due to information access, not architecture. There is no single-model variant (e.g., logistic regression on the same raw sequence features, or an MLP on raw sequences) to isolate the temporal modeling contribution.

The CNN is described as processing 'temporal sequences of treatments (procedures, medications) as feature extractors, feeding into an LSTM recurrent layer.' A 1D CNN with kernel size 3 extracts local windowed patterns before the LSTM sees them. This means the effective input to the LSTM is not the raw sequence but local convolutional summaries. Whether this design actually captures the irregular temporal spacing typical of clinical event sequences is not addressed — clinical visits are not uniformly spaced, and a fixed-size convolutional kernel applied to an event sequence ignores the time gap between events. Events packed into a short interval are treated identically to the same events spread across months.

Department-level readmission baseline rates are computed from training data and added as features to both models. This is a target-encoding-style feature: it uses training-set aggregates of the readmission label. If this feature is not computed via proper leave-one-out or nested encoding, it will encode the exact training label distribution into both models. Even if properly computed, the justification says it allows both models to 'learn department-specific risk adjustments' — but this feature interacts differently with logistic regression (linear adjustment) versus the neural model (learned nonlinear interaction), creating another asymmetry in how each model can exploit the same information.
all_issues_raised: The CNN-RNN receives full temporal sequences while logistic regression receives only engineered summaries — the experiment cannot isolate whether performance differences come from temporal architecture or raw information access.

The CNN uses fixed-size convolutional kernels on clinical event sequences that are irregularly spaced in time; this ignores the clinical meaning of time gaps between events, potentially limiting the model's ability to capture deterioration dynamics.

Department-level readmission rates computed from training data constitute a form of target encoding; this feature interacts asymmetrically with logistic regression vs. neural models, introducing a hidden advantage for the more flexible model.
all_issues_adjudicated: The CNN-RNN receives full temporal sequences while logistic regression receives only engineered summaries — the experiment cannot isolate whether performance differences come from temporal architecture or raw information access.

The CNN uses fixed-size convolutional kernels on clinical event sequences that are irregularly spaced in time; this ignores the clinical meaning of time gaps between events, potentially limiting the model's ability to capture deterioration dynamics.

Department-level readmission rates computed from training data constitute a form of target encoding; this feature interacts asymmetrically with logistic regression vs. neural models, introducing a hidden advantage for the more flexible model.
